quiz.question: The soldiers were decorated for their bravery in the field. What does 'decorate' mean here?
options: To honor with an official medal or award
To recognize publicly for outstanding conduct
To receive formal praise from a senior commander
To be commended in dispatches for acts of courage
quiz.answer: To honor with an official medal or award
quiz.explanation:
To decorate means to honor with a medal — to award an official military distinction.
• decorate = to award a military medal; to adorn with ornament
• From Latin: 'decorare' — to adorn
• 'Decorated veteran', 'decorated for valor' — the distinction of a formal award

quiz.question: The surgeon perforated the appendix accidentally, causing a serious complication. What does 'perforate' mean?
options: To make a hole or holes through something
To puncture an organ during a surgical procedure
To cut through tissue causing an unintended opening
To breach the wall of an internal organ
quiz.answer: To make a hole or holes through something
quiz.explanation:
To perforate means to make holes through — to pierce with one or more holes.
• perforate = to make a hole or series of holes through
• From Latin: 'perforare' — to bore through
• 'Perforated stamp', 'perforated ulcer' — pierced through

quiz.question: A cold shower in the morning invigorated her for the day ahead. What does 'invigorate' mean?
options: To give fresh energy and vitality
To prepare the body for physical exertion
To wake the mind and sharpen concentration
To produce a stimulating physical sensation
quiz.answer: To give fresh energy and vitality
quiz.explanation:
To invigorate means to fill with energy — to make strong and lively.
• invigorate = to give strength and energy; to animate
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'vigorem' (vigor)
• 'An invigorating walk', 'invigorate debate' — bringing in energy

quiz.question: Several measures were introduced to ameliorate the conditions in the overcrowded prison. What does 'ameliorate' mean?
options: To make something bad less severe — to improve
To address the concerns raised by an official inspection
To reduce the number of prisoners held beyond capacity
To upgrade facilities in line with legal requirements
quiz.answer: To make something bad less severe — to improve
quiz.explanation:
To ameliorate means to improve — to make a bad situation better.
• ameliorate = to make something bad less severe; to improve
• From Latin: 'ameliorare' — to make better
• 'Ameliorate suffering', 'ameliorate conditions' — improving without necessarily fixing

quiz.question: The patient's condition deteriorated rapidly overnight. What does 'deteriorate' mean?
options: To become progressively worse
To reach a level where emergency intervention was needed
To decline at a rate faster than the doctors had anticipated
To worsen in a way that altered the treatment plan significantly
quiz.answer: To become progressively worse
quiz.explanation:
To deteriorate means to worsen — to fall into a worse state over time.
• deteriorate = to become progressively worse
• From Latin: 'deteriorare' — to make worse
• 'Deteriorating health', 'deteriorating relations' — steadily getting worse

quiz.question: A statue was erected to commemorate the victims of the disaster. What does 'commemorate' mean?
options: To honor and preserve the memory of someone or something
To mark the anniversary of a tragic event with a memorial
To build a structure as a permanent record of an event
To formally acknowledge the suffering of those affected
quiz.answer: To honor and preserve the memory of someone or something
quiz.explanation:
To commemorate means to remember and honor — to mark something in lasting memory.
• commemorate = to preserve the memory of; to honor with a memorial
• From Latin: 'commemorare' — to bring to mind
• 'Commemorate an anniversary', 'commemorative stamp' — keeping memory alive

quiz.question: The scandal reflected badly on the entire corporate culture of the firm. What does 'corporate' mean?
options: Relating to a large company — of or belonging to a corporation
Characteristic of the management practices of the firm
Shared across an organization rather than belonging to individuals
Typical of large organizations operating in financial markets
quiz.answer: Relating to a large company — of or belonging to a corporation
quiz.explanation:
Corporate means relating to a corporation — shared by all members of an organization.
• corporate = of or relating to a corporation; shared collectively
• From Latin: 'corporare' — to form into a body
• 'Corporate culture', 'corporate responsibility' — the whole organization

quiz.question: The report incorporated feedback from over 200 stakeholders. What does 'incorporate' mean?
options: To include something as part of a larger whole
To use stakeholder comments to improve the final document
To take external input and reflect it in the published version
To add the views of interested parties to an official report
quiz.answer: To include something as part of a larger whole
quiz.explanation:
To incorporate means to include as part of — to take in and make part of a larger body.
• incorporate = to include as part of; to form a legal body
• From Latin: 'incorporare' — to embody
• 'Incorporate feedback', 'incorporated company' — including within a body

quiz.question: The electorate voted overwhelmingly for the incumbent. What is the electorate?
options: All the people entitled to vote in an election
The registered voters who turned out at an election
The population of a country in a democratic system
Those who cast their votes in a particular election
quiz.answer: All the people entitled to vote in an election
quiz.explanation:
The electorate is the body of people eligible to vote — all those with the franchise.
• electorate = all the people entitled to vote
• From 'elector' — one who votes
• 'The electorate rejected the party' = the voters as a whole

quiz.question: He prated on about his achievements for so long that everyone stopped listening. What does 'prate' mean?
options: To talk at length in a tedious, self-important way
To speak without pausing in a way that bored his audience
To repeat the same points in different words
To talk about himself at greater length than the occasion merited
quiz.answer: To talk at length in a tedious, self-important way
quiz.explanation:
To prate means to chatter — to talk at excessive length in a trivial or self-important way.
• prate = to talk at length and tediously
• From Middle Dutch: 'praten' — to chatter
• 'Stop prating' = stop talking emptily; similar to 'prattle'

quiz.question: Critics underrated the film on release, but it became a classic. What does 'underrate' mean?
options: To judge as less good or important than something actually is
To give a poor review based on expectations not being met
To assess unfavorably because the genre was not respected
To dismiss a work that later generations would recognize as significant
quiz.answer: To judge as less good or important than something actually is
quiz.explanation:
To underrate means to underestimate — to judge something as less valuable than it is.
• underrate = to rate too low; to fail to appreciate
• From 'under-' + 'rate'
• 'An underrated film' = better than its reputation suggests

quiz.question: The knife had a serrated edge ideal for cutting bread. What does 'serrate' mean?
options: Having a saw-like edge with a series of notches
Sharpened so that it can cut through tough crusts
Having an edge designed to grip rather than slice
Toothed in a way that prevents slipping while cutting
quiz.answer: Having a saw-like edge with a series of notches
quiz.explanation:
Serrate means having a jagged edge — notched like a saw.
• serrate = having a series of tooth-like projections along the edge
• From Latin: 'serratus' — saw-toothed
• 'Serrated knife', 'serrated leaf edge' — the toothed, saw-like pattern

quiz.question: An independent panel was appointed to arbitrate the labor dispute. What does 'arbitrate' mean?
options: To act as an independent judge to settle a dispute
To make a binding decision on behalf of both parties
To hear both sides of a dispute and issue a ruling
To resolve a disagreement by applying agreed principles
quiz.answer: To act as an independent judge to settle a dispute
quiz.explanation:
To arbitrate means to judge and decide — to act as an impartial arbitrator.
• arbitrate = to act as an arbitrator; to make an authoritative judgment
• From Latin: 'arbitrare' — to act as judge
• 'Arbitrate a dispute', 'binding arbitration' — formal impartial judgment

quiz.question: The intelligence agency had infiltrated the criminal organization. What does 'infiltrate' mean?
options: To secretly enter and become part of an organization
To place an agent inside a group without being detected
To gather intelligence by pretending to be a member
To penetrate a network through a covert operation
quiz.answer: To secretly enter and become part of an organization
quiz.explanation:
To infiltrate means to enter secretly — to penetrate covertly.
• infiltrate = to enter gradually and secretly
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'filtrare' (to filter through)
• 'Infiltrate a network', 'radical ideas infiltrating' — entering from within

quiz.question: She asked everyone to concentrate during the final minutes of the exam. What does 'concentrate' mean?
options: To focus attention fully on the task in hand
To work as efficiently as possible in the time remaining
To review answers carefully before submitting the paper
To give the exam one's full and undivided attention
quiz.answer: To focus attention fully on the task in hand
quiz.explanation:
To concentrate means to focus — to direct all attention toward one thing.
• concentrate = to focus attention or effort; to increase in density
• From Latin: 'concentrare' — to bring to a center
• 'Concentrate on', 'concentrated solution' — intensity of focus or substance

quiz.question: The court ordered the firm's assets to be sequestrated pending investigation. What does 'sequestrate' mean?
options: To take legal possession of property as a precaution
To freeze the accounts of a company under investigation
To confiscate assets until a court case has been resolved
To appoint a receiver to manage the assets of a company
quiz.answer: To take legal possession of property as a precaution
quiz.explanation:
To sequestrate means to seize by legal authority — to take possession of property.
• sequestrate = to take possession of assets by legal authority
• From Latin: 'sequestrare' — to put in safekeeping
• 'Sequestrated assets', 'sequestration order' — legal seizure

quiz.question: Thousands gathered in the capital to demonstrate against the new law. What does 'demonstrate' mean here?
options: To protest publicly — to march or gather to express opposition
To gather in large numbers to show disapproval of a policy
To exercise the right to express collective public opinion
To use public assembly as a way of putting pressure on government
quiz.answer: To protest publicly — to march or gather to express opposition
quiz.explanation:
To demonstrate means to take part in a public protest — to march and show opposition.
• demonstrate = to show; to take part in a public protest
• From Latin: 'demonstrare' — to point out
• 'Demonstrate a skill', 'demonstrate against a law' — showing or protesting

quiz.question: The teacher remonstrated with the students about their disruptive behavior. What does 'remonstrate' mean?
options: To protest or complain firmly to someone in authority
To make clear that a behavior would not be tolerated
To address poor behavior directly and firmly
To warn students about the consequences of continued disruption
quiz.answer: To protest or complain firmly to someone in authority
quiz.explanation:
To remonstrate means to argue or protest — to make a firm objection.
• remonstrate = to make a protest; to argue earnestly against
• From Latin: 'remonstrare' — to point out again
• 'Remonstrate with someone' = confront them firmly with an objection

quiz.question: Despite hours of negotiation, he remained obdurate. What does 'obdurate' mean?
options: Stubbornly refusing to change one's position
Too committed to a position to make any concessions
Unwilling to engage in meaningful negotiation
Firm enough in his views to resist all forms of pressure
quiz.answer: Stubbornly refusing to change one's position
quiz.explanation:
Obdurate means stubbornly inflexible — hardened against persuasion.
• obdurate = stubbornly refusing to change; inflexible
• From Latin: 'obdurare' — to harden
• 'Obdurate refusal', 'obdurate sinner' — hardened against all appeals

quiz.question: Years of hardship had indurated him against self-pity. What does 'indurate' mean?
options: To make hard or callous — to harden emotionally
To develop resilience through exposure to difficulty
To become incapable of feeling sympathy for oneself
To harden one's character through prolonged suffering
quiz.answer: To make hard or callous — to harden emotionally
quiz.explanation:
To indurate means to harden — to make tough and unfeeling through experience.
• indurate = to harden; to make callous
• From Latin: 'indurare' — to harden
• Close to 'obdurate'; 'indurate' emphasizes the hardening process

quiz.question: The president was inaugurated in a ceremony watched by millions. What does 'inaugurate' mean?
options: To formally introduce someone to an official position
To take the oath of office before assuming presidential duties
To be installed as the country's new head of state
To begin a term in office through a formal public ceremony
quiz.answer: To formally introduce someone to an official position
quiz.explanation:
To inaugurate means to install formally — to begin officially with a ceremony.
• inaugurate = to introduce officially; to begin formally
• From Latin: 'inaugurare' — to take omens (before installing a leader)
• 'Inaugural speech', 'inaugurate a building' — formal start

quiz.question: She expected a salary commensurate with her experience. What does 'commensurate' mean?
options: Proportionate and appropriate in relation to something
In line with market rates for someone at her level
Reflecting the experience she had accumulated
Sufficient to compensate for years of lower earnings, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Proportionate and appropriate in relation to something
quiz.explanation:
Commensurate means proportionate — corresponding in size or degree.
• commensurate = in proportion; corresponding in measure
• From Latin: 'commensuratus' — measured together
• 'Commensurate with experience' = appropriately matched to it

quiz.question: The penalty was incommensurate with the minor violation. What does 'incommensurate' mean?
options: Not proportionate — out of scale with what is appropriate
Much harsher than the rules permitted for such an offense, as the evidence showed
Exceeding what any reasonable authority would impose
Applied inconsistently compared to similar cases
quiz.answer: Not proportionate — out of scale with what is appropriate
quiz.explanation:
Incommensurate means disproportionate — not in keeping with the scale of something.
• incommensurate = not proportionate; lacking a common measure
• Opposite of commensurate
• 'Incommensurate punishment' = punishment that doesn't fit the crime

quiz.question: The heavy rain saturated the ground, making the fields waterlogged. What does 'saturate' mean?
options: To soak something so thoroughly that no more can be absorbed
To cause flooding by exceeding the drainage capacity
To fill the soil with more water than it can hold
To apply water at a rate faster than it can drain away, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To soak something so thoroughly that no more can be absorbed
quiz.explanation:
To saturate means to soak thoroughly — to fill to the maximum capacity.
• saturate = to fill completely; to soak thoroughly
• From Latin: 'saturare' — to fill full
• 'Saturated fat', 'saturated market' — filled to maximum capacity

quiz.question: The dancer gyrated rhythmically to the beat of the music. What does 'gyrate' mean?
options: To rotate or whirl — to move in a spiral or circular motion
To perform energetic and rhythmic movements to music
To spin in a controlled way as part of a dance routine, as the evidence showed
To move the body in circles as a form of expression
quiz.answer: To rotate or whirl — to move in a spiral or circular motion
quiz.explanation:
To gyrate means to move in circles — to rotate or spiral.
• gyrate = to rotate or spiral; to move in a circle
• From Greek: 'gyros' — a circle
• 'Gyrating hips', 'gyrating markets' — circular or oscillating motion

quiz.question: The airline compensated passengers for the delayed flight. What does 'compensate' mean?
options: To pay or give something to make up for a loss
To offer money as required by the terms of the ticket
To settle claims from passengers who were inconvenienced
To provide a financial payment following a service failure
quiz.answer: To pay or give something to make up for a loss
quiz.explanation:
To compensate means to make up for — to give something to offset a loss.
• compensate = to make up for; to pay for loss or inconvenience
• From Latin: 'compensare' — to weigh against
• 'Compensate for a weakness', 'compensation payment' — making up

quiz.question: He continued his insensate pursuit of profit with no regard for the consequences. What does 'insensate' mean?
options: Lacking feeling or rational judgment — recklessly unfeeling
Driven by greed to the exclusion of all other considerations
Too focused on a single goal to notice the harm being caused
Pursuing a goal without pausing to consider the costs
quiz.answer: Lacking feeling or rational judgment — recklessly unfeeling
quiz.explanation:
Insensate means without feeling or sense — unfeeling, irrational, or reckless.
• insensate = lacking physical feeling; utterly senseless
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'sensatus' (endowed with sense)
• 'Insensate rage', 'insensate cruelty' — beyond normal feeling

quiz.question: Market conditions often dictate the pace of business decisions. What does 'dictate' mean?
options: To impose a course of action — to determine what must happen
To influence the timing and direction of decisions
To create the conditions that shape business behavior, as the evidence showed
To force a business to adapt to external pressures
quiz.answer: To impose a course of action — to determine what must happen
quiz.explanation:
To dictate means to determine imperiously — to lay down what must be done.
• dictate = to order; to state with authority what must occur
• From Latin: 'dictare' — to say often, prescribe
• 'Dictate terms', 'market dictates' — prescribing what must happen

quiz.question: After retiring, he vegetated in front of the television and lost all purpose. What does 'vegetate' mean?
options: To live in a passive, inactive way without mental stimulation
To spend retirement without any meaningful activities
To withdraw from society and abandon former interests
To fill time with passive entertainment rather than engagement
quiz.answer: To live in a passive, inactive way without mental stimulation
quiz.explanation:
To vegetate means to live passively — to exist without activity or engagement.
• vegetate = to live a monotonous, passive existence
• From Latin: 'vegetare' — to animate (ironically used for plant-like passivity)
• 'Vegetating on the sofa' = existing without purpose

quiz.question: The dean felicitated the graduates at the end of the ceremony. What does 'felicitate' mean?
options: To congratulate formally
To welcome graduates into their chosen professions
To close a ceremony with encouraging remarks
To offer good wishes for future success
quiz.answer: To congratulate formally
quiz.explanation:
To felicitate means to congratulate — to express congratulations on an occasion.
• felicitate = to wish happiness to; to congratulate
• From Latin: 'felicitare' — to make happy
• 'Felicitations' = formal congratulations

quiz.question: Paramedics resuscitated the patient after his heart stopped. What does 'resuscitate' mean?
options: To revive from unconsciousness or apparent death
To restore a heartbeat after cardiac arrest
To bring a patient back from a life-threatening condition
To use emergency procedures to maintain vital functions
quiz.answer: To revive from unconsciousness or apparent death
quiz.explanation:
To resuscitate means to revive — to bring back from unconsciousness or near death.
• resuscitate = to revive from unconsciousness or clinical death
• From Latin: 'resuscitare' — to stir up again
• 'CPR resuscitates', 'resuscitate a plan' — bringing back to life

quiz.question: She meditated for thirty minutes each morning before work. What does 'meditate' mean?
options: To engage in contemplative thought or mental stillness
To use a relaxation technique to prepare for the day, as the evidence showed
To practice a form of mental discipline regularly
To sit quietly and focus the mind as a daily ritual
quiz.answer: To engage in contemplative thought or mental stillness
quiz.explanation:
To meditate means to engage in deep, focused thought — or to practice mental stillness.
• meditate = to think deeply; to practice mental quietude
• From Latin: 'meditari' — to think over
• 'Meditate on a problem', 'meditation practice' — focused contemplation

quiz.question: The prosecution argued the attack was premeditated. What does 'premeditate' mean?
options: To plan and think through in advance
To have decided to act before the event took place
To carry out an act with preparation done beforehand
To commit an offense that had been planned before execution
quiz.answer: To plan and think through in advance
quiz.explanation:
To premeditate means to plan beforehand — to think out in advance.
• premeditate = to think out and plan an action beforehand
• From Latin: 'prae-' + 'meditari'
• 'Premeditated murder' = planned in advance — more serious than impulsive acts

quiz.question: She cogitated for a long time before giving her answer. What does 'cogitate' mean?
options: To think carefully and at length
To weigh up all options before committing to one
To reason systematically through a complex question
To reflect quietly before speaking
quiz.answer: To think carefully and at length
quiz.explanation:
To cogitate means to think deeply — to engage in prolonged, careful reasoning.
• cogitate = to meditate or ponder at length
• From Latin: 'cogitare' — to think
• 'Cogitate on a problem' = give it serious, extended thought

quiz.question: The philosopher excogitated a novel solution no one had considered before. What does 'excogitate' mean?
options: To think out carefully — to devise by thinking
To produce an original idea through intense intellectual work
To solve a problem by applying rigorous philosophical analysis
To construct a theory through systematic reasoning
quiz.answer: To think out carefully — to devise by thinking
quiz.explanation:
To excogitate means to think out fully — to devise or work out by careful reasoning.
• excogitate = to think out; to devise by careful thought
• From Latin: 'excogitare' — to think out
• More intense than cogitate — excogitation produces a result

quiz.question: The programme aimed to rehabilitate offenders so they could reenter society. What does 'rehabilitate' mean?
options: To restore to health or normal functioning — to reform
To provide education and training during a prison sentence
To prepare people for life after incarceration
To reduce reoffending through structured intervention
quiz.answer: To restore to health or normal functioning — to reform
quiz.explanation:
To rehabilitate means to restore — to help someone return to a healthy or functional state.
• rehabilitate = to restore to good condition; to reform
• From Latin: 're-' + 'habilitare' (to enable)
• 'Rehabilitate an offender', 'rehabilitate an injury' — restoring capacity

quiz.question: The tropical disease debilitated him for months. What does 'debilitate' mean?
options: To make weak — to reduce the strength of
To produce symptoms severe enough to prevent normal activity
To damage health so significantly that recovery took months
To affect physical and mental capacity through illness
quiz.answer: To make weak — to reduce the strength of
quiz.explanation:
To debilitate means to weaken — to impair health or strength.
• debilitate = to make weak; to impair the strength of
• From Latin: 'debilitare' — to weaken
• 'Debilitating illness', 'debilitating heat' — reducing capacity severely

quiz.question: The resignation precipitated a crisis within the government. What does 'precipitate' mean?
options: To cause something to happen suddenly or prematurely
To trigger an event that had been building for some time
To force a sequence of events that no one had anticipated
To create a political problem through a sudden departure
quiz.answer: To cause something to happen suddenly or prematurely
quiz.explanation:
To precipitate means to cause suddenly — to bring about prematurely.
• precipitate = to cause to happen suddenly; to throw headlong
• From Latin: 'praecipitare' — to hurl down headlong
• 'Precipitate a crisis', 'precipitate action' — headlong speed

quiz.question: Her heart palpitated wildly when she saw the exam results. What does 'palpitate' mean?
options: To beat rapidly and irregularly — to throb with agitation
To respond physically to a strong emotional stimulus
To race in a way that caused physical discomfort
To beat faster and harder than would normally be expected, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To beat rapidly and irregularly — to throb with agitation
quiz.explanation:
To palpitate means to throb — to beat rapidly and noticeably, especially the heart.
• palpitate = to beat rapidly and irregularly; to throb
• From Latin: 'palpitare' — to throb
• 'Heart palpitations' = rapid, irregular heartbeat

quiz.question: The ancient potentate ruled his kingdom through absolute authority. What is a potentate?
options: A monarch or ruler with great and absolute power
A hereditary ruler of a kingdom in the ancient world
A sovereign whose authority was not limited by law
A ruler who governed without any form of elected assembly
quiz.answer: A monarch or ruler with great and absolute power
quiz.explanation:
A potentate is a powerful ruler — a monarch or sovereign with absolute authority.
• potentate = a monarch or despot with great power
• From Latin: 'potentatus' — power, rule
• 'A local potentate', 'an oriental potentate' — great and often absolute power

quiz.question: The professor annotated the text with explanatory notes in the margins. What does 'annotate' mean?
options: To add explanatory notes to a text
To correct errors found in a published work
To mark passages of interest while reading
To prepare a text for publication by adding commentary
quiz.answer: To add explanatory notes to a text
quiz.explanation:
To annotate means to add notes — to furnish a text with explanatory comments.
• annotate = to add notes of explanation or comment to a text
• From Latin: 'annotare' — to note down
• 'Annotated bibliography', 'annotate code' — adding explanatory information

quiz.question: The idea gestated for years before finally becoming a finished novel. What does 'gestate' mean?
options: To develop slowly toward completion
To occupy a writer's mind for an extended period
To be worked on privately before being shared
To take shape over a long period of creative work
quiz.answer: To develop slowly toward completion
quiz.explanation:
To gestate means to develop gradually — to grow and mature toward completion.
• gestate = to carry during pregnancy; to develop slowly
• From Latin: 'gestare' — to carry
• 'Gestate a plan' = allow an idea to develop in the mind over time

quiz.question: He died intestate, leaving his family to fight over his estate. What does 'intestate' mean?
options: Without having made a valid will
Without having allocated assets to specific heirs
Without providing clear instructions for distributing the estate
Without informing family members of his intentions
quiz.answer: Without having made a valid will
quiz.explanation:
Intestate means without a will — dying without having made a valid will.
• intestate = dying without having made a will
• From Latin: 'intestatus' — without a will
• 'Intestacy rules' determine how an estate is distributed without a will

quiz.question: The new principal was instated at the start of the autumn term. What does 'instate' mean?
options: To formally establish in a position or office
To appoint to a role and introduce to the institution
To replace a departing colleague through an official process
To begin a new appointment after a formal selection
quiz.answer: To formally establish in a position or office
quiz.explanation:
To instate means to install formally — to place in a position officially.
• instate = to establish in an office or position
• From 'in-' + 'state' (to place)
• 'Reinstated', 'instated as director' — placed in the position

quiz.question: He was condemned as an apostate for renouncing his faith. What is an apostate?
options: A person who abandons a religion or belief they previously held
A person who converts to a different religious tradition
Someone who publicly criticizes the religion they once followed, as the evidence showed
A former believer whose doubts led them to leave the faith
quiz.answer: A person who abandons a religion or belief they previously held
quiz.explanation:
An apostate is someone who abandons a belief — especially a religion.
• apostate = a person who renounces a religion or belief
• From Greek: 'apostates' — one who defects
• 'Apostasy' = the act; 'apostate' = the person

quiz.question: He understated the severity of the problem to avoid alarming his colleagues. What does 'understate' mean?
options: To describe something as less significant than it actually is
To present a problem in a less alarming way than was accurate, as the evidence showed
To use language that played down the seriousness of events
To withhold the full extent of a problem from those affected
quiz.answer: To describe something as less significant than it actually is
quiz.explanation:
To understate means to represent as less than it is — to minimize in description.
• understate = to state with less emphasis than the facts deserve
• Opposite of overstate
• 'British understatement', 'understate the risks' — deliberate minimization

quiz.question: The report overstated the risk to avoid complacency. What does 'overstate' mean?
options: To represent something as greater than it actually is
To exaggerate a risk in order to produce a stronger response
To present a worst-case scenario as the most likely outcome
To describe dangers in stronger terms than the evidence supports
quiz.answer: To represent something as greater than it actually is
quiz.explanation:
To overstate means to exaggerate — to present something as more than it actually is.
• overstate = to state with more emphasis than is justified
• Opposite of understate
• 'Overstate one's case', 'overstate the danger' — more than the truth allows

quiz.question: The virus mutated in ways that made it more resistant to treatment. What does 'mutate' mean?
options: To undergo a change in genetic structure
To develop resistance by altering its molecular makeup
To evolve through random genetic changes over generations
To change its structure in response to environmental pressure
quiz.answer: To undergo a change in genetic structure
quiz.explanation:
To mutate means to undergo change — especially a change in genetic material.
• mutate = to undergo or cause mutation; to change in form
• From Latin: 'mutare' — to change
• 'Mutation', 'mutant' — altered by change in genetic structure

quiz.question: Surgeons had to amputate the injured leg to save the patient's life. What does 'amputate' mean?
options: To surgically remove a limb or other body part
To perform an emergency operation removing a damaged limb
To remove a limb because infection had made it unsaveable
To carry out surgery that removed a body part beyond repair
quiz.answer: To surgically remove a limb or other body part
quiz.explanation:
To amputate means to cut off surgically — to remove a limb by surgery.
• amputate = to remove a limb by surgical operation
• From Latin: 'amputare' — to cut around
• 'Amputate a leg', 'amputation' — removal of a limb

quiz.question: Residents were ordered to evacuate as the fire spread toward the town. What does 'evacuate' mean?
options: To leave a place urgently because of danger
To follow emergency instructions to leave a threatened area
To move to safety ahead of an advancing emergency
To clear the area under an official order to leave
quiz.answer: To leave a place urgently because of danger
quiz.explanation:
To evacuate means to leave — to move away from a dangerous place.
• evacuate = to leave a dangerous place; to empty of people
• From Latin: 'evacuare' — to empty
• 'Evacuate a building', 'evacuation route' — emergency departure

quiz.question: The signal was attenuated by the thick walls. What does 'attenuate' mean?
options: To reduce in strength, force, or value
To lose power as it passed through a material
To decrease in intensity due to interference
To weaken as a result of the medium it traveled through
quiz.answer: To reduce in strength, force, or value
quiz.explanation:
To attenuate means to weaken — to reduce in force or intensity.
• attenuate = to reduce in thickness or strength; to weaken
• From Latin: 'attenuare' — to make thin
• 'Attenuated signal', 'attenuated virus' — weakened or thinned

quiz.question: The defendant's lawyer cited extenuating circumstances to reduce the sentence. What does 'extenuate' mean?
options: To lessen the seriousness of an offense by partial justification
To reduce the guilt of an accused through mitigating factors, as the evidence showed
To present circumstances that explained but did not excuse
To persuade a court that a lesser penalty was appropriate
quiz.answer: To lessen the seriousness of an offense by partial justification
quiz.explanation:
To extenuate means to reduce the severity — to lessen blame by circumstance.
• extenuate = to make an offense seem less serious
• From Latin: 'extenuare' — to make thin
• 'Extenuating circumstances' = factors that reduce moral blame

quiz.question: She insinuated that his success had been built on dishonesty. What does 'insinuate' mean?
options: To suggest something negative indirectly and craftily
To cast doubt on someone's reputation without direct accusation
To imply a fault without being willing to state it plainly
To use language that conveyed a criticism without expressing it
quiz.answer: To suggest something negative indirectly and craftily
quiz.explanation:
To insinuate means to hint subtly — to imply something unpleasant without stating it.
• insinuate = to suggest in an indirect, sly way
• From Latin: 'insinuare' — to wind oneself into
• 'Insinuate dishonesty', 'insinuate oneself' — getting in by subtle, winding means

quiz.question: She refused to equate financial success with personal happiness. What does 'equate' mean?
options: To consider one thing as equivalent to another
To treat two different concepts as if they were the same
To draw a direct correspondence between two things
To accept that two distinct ideas produce the same outcome
quiz.answer: To consider one thing as equivalent to another
quiz.explanation:
To equate means to regard as equal or equivalent — to treat as the same.
• equate = to consider to be equal or equivalent
• From Latin: 'aequare' — to make equal
• 'Don't equate X with Y' = they are not the same thing

quiz.question: The supplies were adequate but left little margin for error. What does 'adequate' mean?
options: Sufficient for the required purpose — just enough
Available in the quantities needed for the operation
Enough to meet basic needs with nothing left over
Meeting the minimum standard without exceeding it
quiz.answer: Sufficient for the required purpose — just enough
quiz.explanation:
Adequate means sufficient — just enough for the purpose, but not more.
• adequate = enough for a specific purpose; satisfactory but not impressive
• From Latin: 'adaequatus' — made equal to
• 'Barely adequate', 'adequate provision' — meets the need, no more

quiz.question: He was infatuated with her and could think of nothing else. What does 'infatuate' mean?
options: To inspire an intense but unreasoning passion
To feel such strong attraction that judgment was clouded
To develop a consuming interest that excluded all others
To become obsessed with someone in a way that was unhealthy
quiz.answer: To inspire an intense but unreasoning passion
quiz.explanation:
To infatuate means to inspire an irrational passion — to make foolishly in love.
• infatuate = to cause intense but transient passion; to make foolish
• From Latin: 'infatuare' — to make a fool of
• 'Infatuation' = the feeling; intense but often short-lived

quiz.question: The alarm was actuated by a sensor detecting rising temperature. What does 'actuate' mean?
options: To put into action — to trigger or activate
To detect the conditions that trigger an alarm to sound
To cause a device to respond to a stimulus
To enable a mechanical system to begin operating
quiz.answer: To put into action — to trigger or activate
quiz.explanation:
To actuate means to trigger into action — to set a mechanism in motion.
• actuate = to put into action; to motivate
• From Latin: 'actuare' — to put in action
• 'Actuate a mechanism', 'actuated by greed' — the trigger that sets things moving

quiz.question: Temperatures fluctuated wildly through the week. What does 'fluctuate' mean?
options: To change irregularly — to vary up and down
To rise and fall in a pattern that was difficult to predict
To shift between extremes without settling at a stable level
To vary enough that planning became difficult
quiz.answer: To change irregularly — to vary up and down
quiz.explanation:
To fluctuate means to vary irregularly — to shift back and forth unpredictably.
• fluctuate = to rise and fall irregularly
• From Latin: 'fluctuare' — to wave
• 'Fluctuating prices', 'fluctuating mood' — irregular ups and downs

quiz.question: The policy perpetuated inequality by favoring those already advantaged. What does 'perpetuate' mean?
options: To cause something to continue indefinitely
To ensure that a problem was passed on to future generations
To maintain a situation that should have been corrected
To extend the duration of a harmful practice
quiz.answer: To cause something to continue indefinitely
quiz.explanation:
To perpetuate means to cause to continue — to make permanent or ongoing.
• perpetuate = to make perpetual; to preserve from extinction
• From Latin: 'perpetuare' — to make permanent
• 'Perpetuate a myth', 'perpetuate inequality' — keeping going when it should stop

quiz.question: The animals gradually habituated to the presence of researchers. What does 'habituate' mean?
options: To become accustomed to something through repeated exposure
To lose the fear of an unfamiliar stimulus over time
To adjust to a presence that was initially alarming
To adapt behavior in response to a repeated experience, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To become accustomed to something through repeated exposure
quiz.explanation:
To habituate means to become used to — to accustom through repetition.
• habituate = to accustom; to become used to by repeated exposure
• From Latin: 'habituare' — to bring into a condition
• 'Habituated to noise' = no longer troubled by it

quiz.question: The slim fit of the jacket accentuated his broad shoulders. What does 'accentuate' mean?
options: To make more noticeable — to emphasize
To draw attention to a physical feature through clothing
To enhance a quality that was already present
To complement a physical characteristic through design
quiz.answer: To make more noticeable — to emphasize
quiz.explanation:
To accentuate means to emphasize — to make a feature more prominent or visible.
• accentuate = to make more prominent; to emphasize
• From Latin: 'accentuare' — to accent
• 'Accentuate the positive', 'accentuate a feature' — bringing out more strongly

quiz.question: Archaeologists excavated the site over three seasons. What does 'excavate' mean?
options: To dig out carefully to expose what lies beneath
To remove earth and debris from around buried artefacts
To conduct a systematic search of a buried site
To uncover the remains of a past civilization by digging
quiz.answer: To dig out carefully to expose what lies beneath
quiz.explanation:
To excavate means to dig out — to unearth by systematic removal of material.
• excavate = to dig out and remove; to unearth
• From Latin: 'excavare' — to hollow out
• 'Excavate a trench', 'excavate a site' — digging to expose

quiz.question: The bomb squad deactivated the device before it could explode. What does 'deactivate' mean?
options: To make something inactive — to render inoperable
To disarm a device that posed a threat to those nearby
To remove the trigger mechanism so detonation was impossible
To neutralize a dangerous item through specialist procedures
quiz.answer: To make something inactive — to render inoperable
quiz.explanation:
To deactivate means to disable — to render inactive or inoperable.
• deactivate = to render inactive; to disable
• From 'de-' + 'activate'
• 'Deactivate a bomb', 'deactivate an account' — removing the ability to function

quiz.question: She cultivated relationships with key figures across the industry over many years. What does 'cultivate' mean?
options: To develop or nurture through care and attention
To build a professional network through sustained contact
To develop connections that proved useful over time
To invest time in building relationships for future benefit
quiz.answer: To develop or nurture through care and attention
quiz.explanation:
To cultivate means to develop through care — to foster growth by attention.
• cultivate = to nurture; to develop; to till land
• From Latin: 'cultivare' — to till
• 'Cultivate crops', 'cultivate a friendship' — tending to produce growth

quiz.question: The performance captivated the audience from the first scene. What does 'captivate' mean?
options: To hold the complete attention and interest of
To impress an audience with a high-quality performance
To produce a reaction of enthusiasm from the start
To hold an audience's attention throughout a long performance
quiz.answer: To hold the complete attention and interest of
quiz.explanation:
To captivate means to fascinate completely — to hold attention as if captured.
• captivate = to attract and hold attention completely
• From Latin: 'captivare' — to take captive
• 'Captivating story', 'captivated by a speaker' — attention fully held

quiz.question: The old theatre was renovated and reopened to great acclaim. What does 'renovate' mean?
options: To restore to good condition through repair and renewal
To modernize a building while preserving its original features
To carry out improvements that extend the life of a building
To make a building fit for use after a period of neglect
quiz.answer: To restore to good condition through repair and renewal
quiz.explanation:
To renovate means to restore — to repair and renew a building or structure.
• renovate = to restore to good condition; to renew
• From Latin: 'renovare' — to make new again
• 'Renovate a kitchen', 'renovated building' — brought back to good condition

quiz.question: The oppressive heat enervated the entire team before the match even began. What does 'enervate' mean?
options: To drain of energy and vitality
To reduce physical performance in extreme conditions
To make physical effort much harder through heat
To affect both mental and physical strength before competition
quiz.answer: To drain of energy and vitality
quiz.explanation:
To enervate means to weaken — to drain someone of energy and strength.
• enervate = to make weak; to sap the strength or vitality of
• From Latin: 'enervare' — to remove the sinews
• Opposite of 'invigorate' — note the confusion risk

quiz.question: He fixated on the same problem for months without finding a solution. What does 'fixate' mean?
options: To focus on something obsessively — to become preoccupied
To return repeatedly to a problem that refused to be solved
To dwell on a difficulty to a degree that interfered with other work
To be unable to move on from a problem despite its resistance
quiz.answer: To focus on something obsessively — to become preoccupied
quiz.explanation:
To fixate means to become obsessively focused — to be preoccupied to an unhealthy degree.
• fixate = to focus obsessively; to become stuck on
• From Latin: 'fixare' — to fix
• 'Fixated on an idea', 'a fixation' — unhealthy or unproductive preoccupation

quiz.question: The committee dismissed him as an effete intellectual with no practical sense. What does 'effete' mean?
options: Weak, over-refined, and lacking vigor
Too academic to function effectively in the real world
Sophisticated to a degree that made him impractical
Out of touch with practical realities because of privileged education
quiz.answer: Weak, over-refined, and lacking vigor
quiz.explanation:
Effete means exhausted of vitality — over-refined, weak, and lacking energy.
• effete = worn out; having lost vigor; over-refined
• From Latin: 'effetus' — exhausted by bearing young
• 'Effete elite', 'effete style' — weak, pampered, lacking real force

quiz.question: As a committed aesthete, she cared deeply about beauty in all forms. What is an aesthete?
options: A person with a strong appreciation for beauty and art
Someone who prioritizes artistic quality above all other values
A person of refined sensibility who lives for aesthetic pleasure
Someone whose judgments are primarily guided by what is beautiful
quiz.answer: A person with a strong appreciation for beauty and art
quiz.explanation:
An aesthete is someone devoted to beauty — especially in art and culture.
• aesthete = a person with highly developed sensitivity to beauty
• From Greek: 'aisthetikos' — of perception
• 'Self-proclaimed aesthete' = someone who cultivates refined taste

quiz.question: The technology became obsolete within a decade of its introduction. What does 'obsolete' mean?
options: No longer in use because something better has replaced it
Too old to function at the level required for modern needs
Replaced by newer systems that performed the same functions
Overtaken by developments that made it no longer suitable
quiz.answer: No longer in use because something better has replaced it
quiz.explanation:
Obsolete means no longer used — superseded by something better or newer.
• obsolete = no longer produced or used; out of date
• From Latin: 'obsolescere' — to fall into disuse
• 'Obsolete technology', 'obsolete word' — superseded and abandoned

quiz.question: Intensive farming depleted the soil of nutrients. What does 'deplete' mean?
options: To reduce significantly by using up the available supply
To remove the substances needed for continued productivity
To exhaust the natural content of something over time
To draw down resources below the level needed for recovery
quiz.answer: To reduce significantly by using up the available supply
quiz.explanation:
To deplete means to use up — to reduce by consumption until little remains.
• deplete = to use up the supply of; to reduce
• From Latin: 'deplere' — to empty
• 'Deplete reserves', 'ozone depletion' — drawing down what was there

quiz.question: After the enormous meal, they sat back, replete and satisfied. What does 'replete' mean?
options: Well-fed — completely full after eating
So satisfied after eating that no more could be consumed
Comfortable and relaxed after an excellent meal
Sated to the point where continued eating would be impossible
quiz.answer: Well-fed — completely full after eating
quiz.explanation:
Replete means full — especially from having eaten plenty.
• replete = filled to satisfaction; abundantly supplied
• From Latin: 'replere' — to fill again
• 'Replete with examples' = abundantly full; 'feeling replete' = stuffed

quiz.question: The judge meted out harsh sentences to all three defendants. What does 'mete out' mean?
options: To dispense or allot — to deal out in measured portions
To issue the maximum sentence allowed under the law
To apply penalties to each defendant in proportion to their guilt
To hand down sentences after considering all the evidence
quiz.answer: To dispense or allot — to deal out in measured portions
quiz.explanation:
To mete out means to dispense — to deal out in due portions.
• mete = to apportion; to deal out (usually in 'mete out')
• From Old English: 'metan' — to measure
• 'Mete out justice', 'mete out punishment' — dispensing in measured amounts

quiz.question: Sediment accretes slowly on the ocean floor over millions of years. What does 'accrete' mean?
options: To grow by gradual accumulation of external matter
To build up through the repeated settling of particles
To increase in volume through continuous addition
To form layers through the sequential deposit of material
quiz.answer: To grow by gradual accumulation of external matter
quiz.explanation:
To accrete means to grow by accumulation — to increase through addition of material.
• accrete = to grow by gradual external addition
• From Latin: 'accrescere' — to grow
• 'Accretion disk', 'accreted matter' — growing by accumulation

quiz.question: The glands secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream. What does 'secrete' mean?
options: To produce and release a substance from a cell or gland
To generate a chemical that is released into the body
To manufacture a biological compound for internal use
To produce substances that regulate biological processes
quiz.answer: To produce and release a substance from a cell or gland
quiz.explanation:
To secrete means to produce and release — to generate and discharge a substance.
• secrete = to produce and discharge from a gland or cell
• From Latin: 'secernere' — to separate out
• 'Secrete saliva', 'secrete insulin' — biological production and release

quiz.question: We need concrete proposals, not vague aspirations. What does 'concrete' mean here?
options: Specific and definite — having substance and reality
Well-reasoned and supported by evidence
Practical rather than idealistic
Clear enough to be acted on without further definition
quiz.answer: Specific and definite — having substance and reality
quiz.explanation:
Concrete means specific and real — not abstract or vague.
• concrete = definite; not abstract; existing in material form
• From Latin: 'concretus' — grown together
• 'Concrete evidence', 'concrete plans' — real, specific, tangible

quiz.question: The process consisted of five discrete stages, each evaluated separately. What does 'discrete' mean?
options: Individually separate and distinct
Following one after the other in a defined sequence
Independent enough to be assessed in isolation
Separate in function despite being part of the same system
quiz.answer: Individually separate and distinct
quiz.explanation:
Discrete means individually separate — each element distinct and not continuous.
• discrete = individually separate; distinct
• From Latin: 'discretus' — separated
• Confusable with 'discreet' (tactful); 'discrete' = separate, 'discreet' = careful

quiz.question: The kidneys excrete waste products from the blood. What does 'excrete' mean?
options: To discharge waste matter from the body
To filter harmful substances from the blood
To process waste products through the urinary system
To remove toxins from the body through organ function
quiz.answer: To discharge waste matter from the body
quiz.explanation:
To excrete means to eliminate waste — to discharge metabolic waste from the body.
• excrete = to separate and discharge waste matter from the body
• From Latin: 'excernere' — to separate out
• 'Excretion', 'excretory organs' — the organs and processes of waste removal

quiz.question: The demagogue incited the crowd to violence with his inflammatory speech. What does 'incite' mean?
options: To stir up or provoke — to urge to action
To deliver a speech that produced a violent response
To use words that inflamed a crowd beyond restraint
To make statements designed to lead to illegal acts
quiz.answer: To stir up or provoke — to urge to action
quiz.explanation:
To incite means to urge to action — to stir up and provoke.
• incite = to encourage or stir up (usually toward something harmful)
• From Latin: 'incitare' — to set in motion
• 'Incite violence', 'incitement to riot' — provoking others to act

quiz.question: The fugitive was extradited to face trial in the country where the crime was committed. What does 'extradite' mean?
options: To hand over a suspect to the jurisdiction of another country
To transfer a criminal to the country that has requested them, as the evidence showed
To deport a suspect to another country for trial
To fulfill a treaty obligation to return a wanted person
quiz.answer: To hand over a suspect to the jurisdiction of another country
quiz.explanation:
To extradite means to hand over — to deliver a suspected criminal to another jurisdiction.
• extradite = to hand over a person to another country's legal system
• From Latin: 'ex-' + 'tradere' (to hand over)
• 'Extradition treaty', 'extradited to the US' — formal legal transfer

quiz.question: The poet indited a sonnet in her honor. What does 'indite' mean?
options: To compose and write — to put into words
To write a poem addressed to a specific person
To produce a formal literary work in a traditional form
To create a piece of writing as a tribute
quiz.answer: To compose and write — to put into words
quiz.explanation:
To indite means to write or compose — to put into literary form.
• indite = to put into words; to write
• From Old French: 'inditer' — to dictate
• Archaic; 'indited a letter' = composed and wrote it

quiz.question: Her essay drew on recondite sources that most scholars had never encountered. What does 'recondite' mean?
options: Little known — obscure and not easily accessible
Too specialized to be familiar to most researchers
Scholarly in a way that required access to rare archives
Known only to those with deep expertise in the field
quiz.answer: Little known — obscure and not easily accessible
quiz.explanation:
Recondite means obscure — not widely known and difficult to find.
• recondite = little known; abstruse; hidden away
• From Latin: 'recondere' — to put away
• 'Recondite knowledge', 'recondite sources' — hidden from all but specialists

quiz.question: His erudite lecture impressed even the specialists in the room. What does 'erudite' mean?
options: Having or showing great knowledge — learned
Drawing on a depth of reading few others could match
Intellectually impressive in its breadth and depth
Scholarly in a way that earned the respect of experts
quiz.answer: Having or showing great knowledge — learned
quiz.explanation:
Erudite means deeply learned — showing great knowledge and scholarship.
• erudite = having or showing great learning
• From Latin: 'erudire' — to polish, instruct
• 'An erudite scholar', 'erudite prose' — the mark of deep, wide learning

quiz.question: The pencil contains graphite that leaves marks on paper. What is graphite?
options: A soft form of carbon used in pencils and as a lubricant
A mineral that leaves a dark mark on surfaces when rubbed
A carbon-based substance shaped into pencil cores
A naturally occurring material used in writing instruments
quiz.answer: A soft form of carbon used in pencils and as a lubricant
quiz.explanation:
Graphite is a soft form of carbon — used in pencils, lubricants, and batteries.
• graphite = a soft allotrope of carbon used in pencils
• From Greek: 'graphein' — to write
• 'Graphite electrode', 'graphite lubricant' — carbon that writes and slides

quiz.question: The kitchen countertops were made of polished granite. What is granite?
options: A hard igneous rock with a crystalline texture
A durable natural stone quarried for construction
A metamorphic rock prized for its hardness and pattern
A naturally occurring stone used in high-end kitchen design
quiz.answer: A hard igneous rock with a crystalline texture
quiz.explanation:
Granite is a hard igneous rock — formed from slowly cooled magma with visible crystals.
• granite = a coarse-grained igneous rock
• From Italian: 'granito' — grained
• 'Granite countertop', 'granite determination' — the rock's hardness used figuratively

quiz.question: The spark ignited the fuel and the engine roared to life. What does 'ignite' mean?
options: To set on fire — to cause to catch fire
To trigger the combustion process in an engine
To produce a flame through a spark or heat source
To start an engine by introducing a spark to the fuel
quiz.answer: To set on fire — to cause to catch fire
quiz.explanation:
To ignite means to catch fire or cause to catch fire.
• ignite = to set fire to; to catch fire
• From Latin: 'ignire' — to set on fire
• 'Ignite passion', 'ignite a debate' — also figurative for starting something

quiz.question: The earth's fossil fuel resources are finite. What does 'finite' mean?
options: Having a limit — not infinite in amount or extent
Capable of running out if consumed at the current rate
Present in quantities that are measurable and bounded
Available in amounts that cannot be replenished naturally
quiz.answer: Having a limit — not infinite in amount or extent
quiz.explanation:
Finite means having limits — bounded, not endless.
• finite = having an end or limit; not infinite
• From Latin: 'finitus' — limited
• Opposite: infinite; 'finite resources' = they will eventually run out

quiz.question: Can you give me a definite answer by end of day? What does 'definite' mean?
options: Clearly established and not subject to change
A firm commitment that could be relied upon, as the evidence showed
Specific enough to be planned around
Precise enough to end the uncertainty
quiz.answer: Clearly established and not subject to change
quiz.explanation:
Definite means clearly established and certain — not vague or provisional.
• definite = clearly defined; certain
• From Latin: 'definitus' — fixed, determined
• 'Definite answer', 'definite article' — something fixed and clear

quiz.question: She succeeded in spite of every obstacle placed in her way. What does 'in spite of' mean?
options: Without being stopped by — despite
By overcoming every difficulty that was put before her
Even though the obstacles made success much harder
Regardless of what others tried to do to prevent it
quiz.answer: Without being stopped by — despite
quiz.explanation:
In spite of means despite — without being deterred by.
• spite = to hurt or upset; 'in spite of' = notwithstanding
• From Middle Dutch: 'spijt' — contempt
• 'In spite of everything' = despite all opposition

quiz.question: The passage to adulthood was marked by a traditional rite. What is a rite?
options: A ceremonial act or procedure — a religious or social ritual
A formal ceremony that marked an important life transition
A tradition observed by a community at key moments
An act of symbolic significance performed at prescribed times
quiz.answer: A ceremonial act or procedure — a religious or social ritual
quiz.explanation:
A rite is a ceremonial procedure — a formal act with symbolic significance.
• rite = a religious or other ceremonial practice
• From Latin: 'ritus' — usage, ceremony
• 'Rite of passage', 'burial rites' — the ceremony that marks the moment

quiz.question: As a sybarite, he refused to travel anywhere that didn't offer five-star accommodation. What is a sybarite?
options: A person devoted to luxury and sensual pleasure
Someone who insists on the highest standards of comfort
A person too accustomed to comfort to tolerate hardship
Someone who judges everything by its contribution to pleasure
quiz.answer: A person devoted to luxury and sensual pleasure
quiz.explanation:
A sybarite is a person devoted to pleasure and luxury — self-indulgently luxurious.
• sybarite = a person who is self-indulgent in their love of luxury
• From Sybaris — an ancient Greek city in southern Italy proverbial for luxury
• 'Sybaritic lifestyle' = one devoted entirely to pleasure

quiz.question: He called her a hypocrite for preaching frugality while living extravagantly. What is a hypocrite?
options: A person who pretends to have virtues they do not actually possess
Someone who tells others how to live while ignoring the same rules, as the evidence showed
A person whose stated beliefs are contradicted by their behavior
Someone who condemns in others what they practice themselves
quiz.answer: A person who pretends to have virtues they do not actually possess
quiz.explanation:
A hypocrite is someone whose behavior contradicts their stated beliefs.
• hypocrite = a person who pretends to have moral standards they lack
• From Greek: 'hypokrites' — a stage actor
• 'Hypocrisy' = the behavior; 'hypocritical' = the adjective

quiz.question: The speech was full of trite phrases that everyone had heard a hundred times. What does 'trite' mean?
options: Overused to the point of being meaningless
So familiar that they produced no effect on the audience
Borrowed from sources too widely known to impress
Repeated so often they had entirely lost their power
quiz.answer: Overused to the point of being meaningless
quiz.explanation:
Trite means worn out by overuse — too familiar to have any impact.
• trite = lacking originality because of overuse
• From Latin: 'tritus' — worn by rubbing
• 'Trite observation', 'trite phrase' — so often repeated it means nothing

quiz.question: He appeared genuinely contrite when he apologized for his behavior. What does 'contrite' mean?
options: Feeling deep remorse — sincerely sorry
Humble enough to apologize publicly and face the consequences
Aware of having caused harm and visibly troubled by it
Willing to accept any punishment resulting from bad behavior
quiz.answer: Feeling deep remorse — sincerely sorry
quiz.explanation:
Contrite means genuinely repentant — feeling deep remorse.
• contrite = feeling sincere sorrow for wrongdoing; penitent
• From Latin: 'contritus' — worn down
• 'A contrite apology' comes from the heart; contrition = deep remorse

quiz.question: The bank agreed to underwrite the bond issue. What does 'underwrite' mean?
options: To guarantee or financially support a risk or enterprise
To buy all remaining shares if a bond issue fails to sell
To commit to buying any unsold portion of a financial issue
To accept financial responsibility for a transaction
quiz.answer: To guarantee or financially support a risk or enterprise
quiz.explanation:
To underwrite means to guarantee — to accept financial liability for something.
• underwrite = to accept financial liability; to sign and accept liability
• From 'under' + 'write' — originally signing one's name under a policy
• 'Underwrite insurance', 'underwrite a bond' — guaranteeing the financial risk

quiz.question: She lacked the requisite qualifications for the position. What does 'requisite' mean?
options: Required or necessary as a condition
Specified as an essential requirement in the job description
Expected by the employer and necessary for the role
Listed as non-negotiable criteria for candidates
quiz.answer: Required or necessary as a condition
quiz.explanation:
Requisite means necessary — required for a specific purpose.
• requisite = required; necessary
• From Latin: 'requisitus' — sought again
• 'The requisite skills', 'a requisite condition' — must be present

quiz.question: Fluency in Spanish was a prerequisite for the role in Latin America. What is a prerequisite?
options: Something required in advance before something else can happen
A qualification that must be held before a post can be offered, as the evidence showed
A condition that must be satisfied before proceeding
A skill without which the other requirements cannot be met
quiz.answer: Something required in advance before something else can happen
quiz.explanation:
A prerequisite is a prior requirement — something that must be in place beforehand.
• prerequisite = a required prior condition
• From Latin: 'prae-' + 'requisitus'
• 'Prerequisites for enrollment', 'a prerequisite for success' — required first

quiz.question: A company car was one of the perquisites of the senior role. What is a perquisite?
options: A benefit or perk attached to a position beyond basic pay
A financial reward given in addition to a basic salary
An advantage that made a senior position more attractive
An extra that compensated for the demands of a senior role
quiz.answer: A benefit or perk attached to a position beyond basic pay
quiz.explanation:
A perquisite is a perk — an extra benefit enjoyed as a right of office.
• perquisite = a privilege or profit incidental to a regular salary
• From Latin: 'perquisitum' — something sought out
• 'Perquisites of office', 'perks and perquisites' — extras beyond the salary

quiz.question: The jeweller produced a piece of exquisite craftsmanship. What does 'exquisite' mean?
options: Extremely beautiful and of the highest quality
Technically perfect in ways that showed rare skill
Too fine and delicate for anything but the closest inspection
Made with a standard of care that elevated it above the ordinary
quiz.answer: Extremely beautiful and of the highest quality
quiz.explanation:
Exquisite means of the most refined beauty — intensely excellent.
• exquisite = extremely beautiful; intensely felt
• From Latin: 'exquisitus' — carefully sought out
• 'Exquisite pain', 'exquisite taste' — the very finest or most intense of its kind
